paper_title: DOT-MoE: Differentiable Optimal Transport for MoEfication

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: The scaling of Large Language Models (LLMs) has driven significant performance gains but created substantial challenges in inference efficiency. While Mixture of Experts (MoEs) architectures address this by decoupling model size from inference cost, training MoEs from scratch is often unstable and compute intensive. Conversion of pre-trained dense models into sparse MoEs has emerged as an alternative solution; however, existing methods typically rely on heuristic neuron clustering or random splitting to partition the Feed-Forward Network (FFN) into experts. In this work, we propose DOT-MoE, a novel framework that formulates the decomposition of dense layers as a Differentiable Optimal Transport (DOT) problem. Instead of static heuristics, we model neuron assignment as a balanced transport problem, utilizing differentiable Sinkhorn-Knopp iterations to enforce strict expert capacity constraints. Furthermore, we utilize Straight-Through Estimators (STE) to jointly learn the discrete neuron-to-expert assignment and the token-to-expert routing policy end-to-end. Extensive experiments across multiple architectures and benchmarks demonstrate that DOT-MoE significantly outperforms structured pruning, heuristic clustering, and random-split baselines, retaining 90% of the original dense model's performance while reducing active parameters by 50%.

sections.content: The scaling of Large Language Models (LLMs) has driven significant performance gains but created substantial challenges in inference efficiency. While Mixture of Experts (MoEs) architectures address this by decoupling model size from inference cost, training MoEs from scratch is often unstable and compute intensive. Conversion of pre-trained dense models into sparse MoEs has emerged as an alternative solution; however, existing methods typically rely on heuristic neuron clustering or random splitting to partition the Feed-Forward Network (FFN) into experts. In this work, we propose DOT-MoE, novel framework that formulates the decomposition of dense layers as Differentiable Optimal Transport (DOT) problem. Instead of static heuristics, we model neuron assignment as balanced transport problem, utilizing differentiable SinkhornKnopp iterations to enforce strict expert capacity constraints. Furthermore, we utilize StraightThrough Estimators (STE) to jointly learn the discrete neuron-to-expert assignment and the tokento-expert routing policy end-to-end. Extensive experiments across multiple architectures and benchmarks demonstrate that DOT-MoE significantly outperforms structured pruning, heuristic clustering, and random-split baselines, retaining 90% of the original dense models performance while reducing active parameters by 50%. 6 2 0 2 1 ] . [ 1 6 6 6 1 0 . 6 0 6 2 : r 1. Introduction The rapid scaling of Large Language Models (LLMs) has led to remarkable capabilities in natural language understanding and generation. However, this performance comes at prohibitive computational cost. As model dimensions grow, the dense activation patterns of standard Transformers *Equal contribution 1Amazon. Correspondence to: Udbhav Bamba <udbhb@amazon.com>. Proceedings of the 43 rd International Conference on Machine Learning, Seoul, South Korea. PMLR 306, 2026. Copyright 2026 by the author(s). 1 (Vaswani et al., 2017), where every parameter is active for every input token, result in unsustainable inference latency and resource consumption. To address this efficiency bottleneck, Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) (Shazeer et al., 2017; Lepikhin et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2024; Fedus et al., 2022) architectures have emerged as promising solution. By routing tokens to small subset of expert sub-networks, MoEs decouple model size from inference cost. For example, the recent Qwen3-30B-A3B (Yang et al., 2025) MoE architecture comprises total of 30.5B parameters in the network, however, only 3.3B parameters are activated per token during inference. Despite their inference efficiency, training MoE models from scratch is notoriously data-hungry and unstable, often requiring complex load-balancing auxiliaries (Zoph et al., 2022; Fedus et al., 2022). Consequently, new paradigm has gained traction: MoEfication (Zhang et al., 2022), or the conversion of pre-trained dense models into sparse MoEs. This approach leverages the high-quality representations of existing dense checkpoints, transforming the Feed-Forward Network (FFN) in each block into sparse experts to reduce inference FLOPs without the cost of pre-training from scratch. Conceptually, this process can be viewed as form of dynamic structural pruning. Unlike static pruning methods (Frantar & Alistarh, 2023; Sun et al.; Ashkboos et al., 2024; Gao et al., 2024b), which permanently remove parameters and often degrade performance by erasing long-tail knowledge essential for LLMs (Lele et al., 2025), MoEfication retains the full parameter set but activates it selectively. By dynamically pruning the network structure conditioned on the input, it maintains the high capacity of the dense model while achieving the efficiency of sparse execution. The core challenge in this conversion is neuron assignment: determining how to partition the thousands of intermediate neurons in dense FFN into discrete, independent, and functionally coherent experts. Existing approaches to denseto-MoE conversion (Zhu et al., 2024; Qu et al., 2024; Pei et al., 2025) rely largely on heuristic strategies for neuron assignment. While effective, these methods often treat neuron assignment and router training as separate processes. They lack unified, differentiable framework that guarantees balanced expert capacity while simultaneously optimizing for the semantic routing of tokens. DOT-MoE: Differentiable Optimal Transport for MoEfication In this work, we propose DOT-MoE, novel framework that formulates the decomposition of dense layers into experts as Differentiable Optimal Transport (DOT) problem. We enable end-to-end learning of both the expert decomposition and the routing mechanism by using differentiable SinkhornKnopp iterations (Knight, 2008; Sinkhorn & Knopp, 1967). Unlike existing methods (Pei et al., 2025; Zhu et al., 2024; Qu et al., 2024) which freeze the assignment and then train the router, DOT-MoE allows the router and the expert assignment to co-adapt. Instead of relying on static heuristics, we view neuron assignment as balanced transport problem where neurons must be transported to experts under strict capacity constraints. This is achieved with Straight-Through Estimators (STEs) (Bengio et al., 2013) that allow gradients to flow through the discrete assignment decisions. This ensures that experts are not just random collections of neurons, but balanced functional units optimized specifically for the routing policy. Our main contributions are: 1. We introduce an optimal transport framework for denseto-MoE conversion, formulating neuron assignment as balanced transport problem with differentiable Sinkhorn iterations. 2. We develop dual-level assignment mechanism that jointly optimizes neuron-to-expert decomposition and token-to-expert routing via complementary straightthrough estimators, enabling the router and expert structure to co-adapt unlike prior methods that treat these as separate stages. 3. We demonstrate through extensive experiments across three model families (LLaMA-2, LLaMA-3, Qwen2.5) and six benchmarks that DOT-MoE outperforms both structured pruning and existing MoEfication methods, retaining 90% of the original dense models performance at 50% parametric count. 2. Background and Motivation 2.1. Preliminaries: FFN Layers in Transformers The FFN constitutes the majority of parameters in transformer-based language models, typically accounting for approximately two-thirds of total model parameters. It processes hidden states through two-stage projection: = σ(xWgate) (xWup) FFN(x) = HWdown (1) (2) where Rd is the input hidden state, Wgate, Wup Rddffn and Wdown Rdffnd are weight matrices, σ() is an activation function (e.g., SiLU), and denotes elementwise multiplication. 2.2. Existing Approaches for Efficient Inference Reducing the computational cost of large language models while preserving their capabilities remains central challenge. Existing approaches fall into two categories: structured pruning, which permanently removes model components, and dense-to-MoE conversion, which converts dense layers into sparse mixtures of experts. 2.2.1. STRUCTURED PRUNING Structured pruning improves efficiency by permanently removing structured components such as neurons, channels, attention heads, or entire layers from dense LLMs (Wang et al., 2020). Representative methods such as ShortGPT (Men et al., 2025) and SliceGPT (Ashkboos et al., 2024) exploit structural redundancies via layeror subspace-level importance criteria, achieving hardware-friendly sparsity and predictable inference speedups. More recent approaches, notably DISP-LLM (Gao et al., 2024b), substantially improve the accuracy-efficiency trade-off by enabling flexible, dimension-wise structural pruning and consistently outperform prior structured pruning baselines. However, structured pruning irreversibly removes model capacity, which often leads to sharp performance degradation, particularly at high compression ratios. 2.2.2. DENSE-TO-MOE CONVERSION MoEfication (Zhang et al., 2022) introduced the paradigm of converting dense FFN layers into sparse MoE layers, preserving total model capacity while reducing per-token computation. This is achieved by partitioning the dffn intermediate neurons into disjoint expert groups, each containing = dffn/E neurons. learned router Wr REdmodel selects the top-k experts per token: = top-k (cid:0)softmax(xW ), k(cid:1) (3) This sparsification reduces computational cost from O(dffn) to O(k dffn/E) per token. Existing dense-to-MoE methods differ primarily in how they assign neurons to experts: (i) Random Assignment. LLaMA-MoE (Zhu et al., 2024) randomly partitions neurons into experts and relies on extensive continued pre-training to recover performance. While simple, this approach requires substantial computational resources and provides no principled basis for expert specialization. (ii) Weight-based Clustering. LTE and MoEfication (Zhang et al., 2022; Zheng et al., 2024) clusters neurons based on the similarity of their projection weights Wgate and Wup, assuming that neurons with similar input-side weights respond to similar input patterns. (iii) Activation-based Clustering. LLaMA-MoE-v2 (Qu et al., 2024) assigns neurons to experts based on impor2 DOT-MoE: Differentiable Optimal Transport for MoEfication tance estimates derived from activations and gradients. CMoE (Pei et al., 2025) clusters intermediate FFN activations with balanced k-means, grouping neurons by empirical co-activation patterns. 2.3. Limitation: Optimizing Proxies Instead of Outputs Existing approaches share fundamental limitation: they optimize proxies for intermediate representations while neglecting the actual output. Consider Equation 2: the output of the FFN depends on the interaction between intermediate activations and down-projection weights Wdown. Structured pruning methods optimize layer-wise importance scores that ignore this interaction. Dense-to-MoE methods cluster based on input weights, intermediate activations, or co-activation patterns, all of which are proxies that fail to capture how each neuron ultimately contributes to the output. To empirically validate this limitation, we conducted controlled single-layer reconstruction analysis on LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3 (see Appendix A). By isolating the expert construction strategy, we observe that methods relying on input-side statistics (Zhu et al., 2024) or intermediate activations (Qu et al., 2024; Pei et al., 2025) incur mean squared errors ranging from 2 to over 41 higher than our proposed approach. These results confirm that preserving FFN fidelity requires explicitly modeling the neurons contribution to the output, rather than optimizing based on proxies. 3. Method 3.1. Problem Formulation Our goal is to convert dense FFN layer into sparse MoE layer with experts, each containing intermediate neurons such that = dffn, while activating only < experts per token. Crucially, we aim to achieve this conversion without full model fine-tuning, enabling efficient post-hoc sparsification of pretrained dense models. The central challenge is neuron assignment: determining which of the dffn neurons should be grouped into each expert. This problem is combinatorially intractable, with dffn! (s!)E possible balanced partitions. Furthermore, assignment and routing are tightly coupled, i.e., changing which neurons belong to an expert changes what inputs should be routed to it, and vice versa. This interdependence precludes simple two-stage approaches that fix assignments before training routers. We propose to jointly learn the neuron-to-expert assignment and the token-to-expert routing by formulating assignment as an optimal transport problem. The key insight is that neuron assignment can be viewed as transporting mass from neurons to experts: each neuron carries unit mass to be delivered to exactly one expert, while each expert must receive exactly units. The cost of each assignment is determined by how well the resulting MoE reconstructs the dense FFN output. This perspective maps directly to optimal transport (OT), which finds minimum-cost mass redistribution under marginal constraints. This formulation requires three components: 1. differentiable relaxation of the discrete assignment that permits gradient-based optimization. 2. Hard capacity constraints ensuring each expert receives exactly neurons and each neuron is assigned to exactly one expert. 3. An output-aware objective that directly measures deviation from the dense FFN output, not intermediate representations. 3.2. Neuron-to-Expert Assignment via Optimal Transport We first formalize the assignment problem using the language of optimal transport. Definition 3.1 (Optimal Transport Problem). Given source + and target distribution Rn distribution Rm + with (cid:80) cj, and cost matrix Rmn, the optimal transport problem seeks transport plan that minimizes total transportation cost: ri = (cid:80) = argmin MU (r,c) C, (4) where U(r, c) = {M 0 : M1n = r, M1m = c} is the set of valid transport plans (the transportation polytope), and , denotes the Frobenius inner product. For neuron assignment, we set = dffn (neurons) and = (experts), with marginals = 1dffn (each neuron assigned exactly once) and = 1E (each expert receives exactly neurons). Limitation of Standard OT. We denote the resulting soft assignment as Msoft [0, 1]dffnE, computed via log-domain Sinkhorn iterations for numerical stability (see Appendix B.2.1). 3.3. Token-to-Expert Routing In addition to assigning neurons to experts, we must learn which experts to activate for each input token. We parameterize the router as linear projection followed by top-k selection. For input tokens Rnd, the router computes: RnE = XW = softmax(L) RnE Ii = top-k(Pi, k) {1, . . . , n} (8) (9) (10) where Wr REd are learnable router weights and Ii contains the indices of the experts selected for token i. 3.4. Differentiable Assignment and Routing With the affinity matrix governing neuron-to-expert assignment and router weights Wr governing token-to-expert routing, we now describe how to jointly optimize both. Two challenges must be addressed: (1) converting the soft assignment Msoft to discrete expert clusters, and (2) enabling gradient flow through both the discrete assignment and the top-k routing selection. Hard Assignment via Greedy Rounding. The soft assignment Msoft provides fractional neuron-to-expert allocations, but deployment requires discrete assignments. We convert Msoft to binary matrix {0, 1}dffnE via greedy selection: sort all entries of Msoft in descending order, then iteratively assign neuron to expert if neuron is unassigned and expert has capacity remaining. This yields disjoint expert clusters C1, . . . , CE with Ce = s. potential concern is mismatch between soft and hard assignments, e.g., when neurons preferred expert has reached capacity. However, Sinkhorn already accounts for capacity constraints globally, redistributing probability mass when experts are over-demanded. Gradient Estimation via STE. Both the greedy rounding for assignment and the top-k selection for routing are non-differentiable. We employ straight-through estimators (STE) (Bengio et al., 2013) that use hard decisions in the forward pass while routing gradients through the soft counterparts in the backward pass: MSTE = + (Msoft sg(Msoft)) RSTE = + (P sg(P)) (11) (12) where sg() denotes the stop-gradient operator, {0, 1}dffnE is the hard neuron assignment from greedy rounding, and {0, 1}nE is the binary routing mask with Ri,e = 1 iff expert is selected for token i. This allows end-to-end optimization: gradients from the reconstruction loss flow through Msoft to update A, and through to update Wr. 3.5. Alignment Phase During training, we simulate sparse MoE computation by masking the intermediate activations: ˆY = (H (RM))Wdown, where only the neurons belonging to the selected experts contribute to each tokens output (see Appendix E). We jointly optimize the assignment logits and router weights Wr across the network using combination of losses aimed at preserving the residual stream: KL divergence between the pretrained dense teacher and MoE student output distributions, cross-entropy loss on the language modeling objective, and auxiliary losses for MoE training stability. Specifically, we employ router zloss (Zoph et al., 2022) to penalize large router logits and prevent instability, and load balancing loss (Shazeer et al., 2017) to encourage uniform expert utilization and prevent expert collapse. Full details are provided in Appendix D. Once training converges, we extract the final assignment and convert the model into standard MoE architecture with distinct expert FFNs, enabling efficient sparse inference. The same balanced-transport formulation extends directly to multi-head attention by grouping heads into experts; we defer the full formulation and results to Appendix G. We evaluate on three publicly available dense checkpoints: LLaMA-2-7B (Touvron et al., 2023), LLaMA-3-8B (Grattafiori et al., 2024), and Qwen2.57B (Team, 2024). All methods are evaluated using lmevaluation-harness (Gao et al., 2024a) with benchmarks default prompts and standard few-shot settings: ARCChallenge (25-shot) (Clark et al., 2018), Winogrande (5shot) (Sakaguchi et al., 2021), HellaSwag (10-shot) (Zellers et al., 2019), PIQA (0-shot) (Bisk et al., 2020), SciQ (0shot) (Welbl et al., 2017), and BoolQ (32-shot) (Clark et al., 2019). Implementation. DOT-MoE is implemented using PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) and Hugging Face Transformers (Wolf et al., 2020). We freeze the dense model weights during the alignment phase and only train the assignment logits and router weights Wr. Unless stated otherwise, each expert contains = 128 intermediate neurons, yielding = 148, 112, and 86 experts per layer for Qwen2.57B, LLaMA-3-8B, and LLaMA-2-7B, respectively. We use top-k routing with fixed fraction of active experts per token. For our main experiments, we target 25% FFN sparsity which translates to = 37, 28and 22 active experts for Qwen2.5-7B, LLaMA-3-8B, and LLaMA-2-7B respectively. We use AdamW with cosine learning rate decay and linear warmup. All experiments are conducted on 8xH100 GPUs. Full hyperparameters are provided in Appendix B. Training Data. We use Dolmino-mix (OLMo et al., 2025) for the alignment phase as well as for continuous fine-tuning. Alignment is done for 3500 steps which takes < 3 hours on 8xH100 GPUs for the LLaMA3-8B; detailed profiling of Sinkhorn and STE overhead is provided in Appendix H. We exclusively train the assignment logits and router weights Wr in the alignment phase, completely freezing the dense model weights. In order to be consistent with existing works, we further use 1.2B tokens for continuous fine-tuning of the aligned model. We sample the same data from the larger Dolmino-mix for training DOT-MoE as well as the baselines. Baselines. We compare DOT-MoE against broad set of structured pruning, semi-structured pruning and dense-toMoE conversion methods. Structured pruning baselines include LLM-Pruner (Ma et al., 2023), LLM Surgeon (Ouderaa et al., 2023), ShortGPT (Men et al., 2025), SLEB (Song et al., 2024), K-OBD, SliceGPT (Ashkboos et al., 2024), ModeGPT (Lin et al., 2024) and DISP-LLM (Gao et al., 2024b). Semi-structured pruning baselines include Wanda (Sun et al.), SparseGPT (Frantar & Alistarh, 2023) and Pruner-Zero (Dong et al., 2024). Dense-to-MoE baselines include CMoE (Pei et al., 2025), LLaMA-MoE (Zhu et al., 2024) and LLaMA-MoE-v2 (Qu et al., 2024). All baselines are evaluated under comparable sparsity. We justify baseline selection and provide additional comparisons in Appendix I. 4.2. Main Results Comparison with Pruning Methods. Table 1 compares DOT-MoE against structured and semi-structured pruning methods on LLaMA-2 7B at 50% parametric budget. DOTMoE achieves the lowest perplexity (7.99) among all existing methods, outperforming the state-of-the-art DISP-LLM (9.84) by substantial margin. DOT-MoE is also competitive with semi-structured pruning methods which have greater degree of freedom to achieve any target sparsity. This advantage extends to downstream tasks. As shown in Table 3, DOT-MoE demonstrates superior knowledge retention compared to pruning baselines across all three model families. On Qwen2.5-7B, DOT-MoE outperforms DISPLLM (72.3% vs 66.7% average accuracy), confirming that dense-to-MoE conversion is more effective than pruning, as it preserves total model capacity while activating only subset of parameters per token. As shown in Table 3, DOTMoE achieves 61.5% average accuracy on LLaMA-2 7B, substantially outperforming CMoE (44.5%). similar trend can be seen for other models. These results demonstrate that our output-aware expert construction optimizes the trade-off between sparsity and reconstruction fidelity more effectively than activation-based clustering. Impact of Fine-tuning. While DOT-MoE is effective outof-the-box, we investigate the impact of continuous finetuning. As shown in Table 2, fine-tuning DOT-MoE on 1.2B tokens boosts LLaMA-2 7B accuracy from 61.5% to 66.6%, widening the gap against CMoE (51.7%) and LLaMA-MoEv2 (48.1%). On LLaMA-3 8B, DOT-MoE achieves 67.8% with 1.2B tokens and improves to 71.0% when scaled to 7B tokens, outperforming LLaMA-MoE-v2 (66.8%) trained on the exact similar data split. This scaling behavior confirms that DOT-MoE provides superior initialization for sparse models that continues to benefit from additional training data. It is worth noting that DOT-MoE substantially closes the gap between Dense and Sparse model with as few as 1.2B tokens of training. Observation 1: Routing benefits from fine-grained experts up to point beyond which additional experts provide limited returns. 4.3. Ablation Studies 4.3.1. EXPERT GRANULARITY In Figure 1a, we investigate the effect of expert granularity on performance by varying the total number of experts (E {16, 37, 74, 148, 256}) in DOT-MoE on Qwen2.5 7B. One might argue that DOT-MoE exhibits better performance due to higher number of experts. However prior Dense-toMoE methods (Pei et al., 2025; Qu et al., 2024) observed that increasing expert count from 8 to 16 leads to minimal improvement or degraded performance due to increased routing complexity, whereas DOT-MoE maintains stable performance at much higher expert counts. To test this, we trained CMoE model with Qwen2.5-7B backbone. We set the total number of experts to 37 and active experts to 9; and observed >5K WikiText perplexity for CMoE. In case of DOT-MoE, increasing the number of experts initially improves performance, but gains saturate as granularity becomes excessively fine, consistent with observations in 4.3.2. EFFECT OF EXPERT GRANULARITY ON INFERENCE SPEED natural concern with fine-grained experts is inference overhead: does increasing the number of experts slow down generation? To investigate, we benchmark inference throughput using vLLMs fused MoE kernels (Kwon et al., 2023) across four expert configurations (E {8, 16, 74, 148}) while holding active parameters constant at 25% of the FFN. Figure 1c shows throughput (tokens/sec) across batch sizes. Crucially, throughput remains stable as expert count increases. This is because vLLMs fused MoE implementation batches all expert computations into small number of large GEMMs rather than executing each expert separately. The fused weights are stored as Wfused = [W1, . . . , WE] along the expert dimension, and token reordering enables single large matrix multiplication regardless of expert count. Since the total fused intermediate dimension (E s) and ac7 DOT-MoE: Differentiable Optimal Transport for MoEfication (a) Training Loss (b) WikiText PPL (c) HellaSwag Acc Norm Figure 2. Effect of initialization on training dynamics. DOT-MoE starts with substantially lower training loss and WikiText perplexity, maintaining this advantage throughout fine-tuning. This translates to consistently higher downstream accuracy on HellaSwag. tive neurons per token (k s) remain constant, the GEMM sizes and thus throughput are largely unaffected by expert granularity. Observation 2: Fine-grained experts incur no throughput penalty with fused MoE kernels when active parameters are held constant. 4.3.3. EFFECT OF TRAINING SPARSITY. key advantage of our method is the ability to dynamically adjust the number of active experts at inference time, enabling flexible compute-accuracy trade-offs without alignment or fine-tuning. We investigate how the sparsity level used during training affects the models generalization across different inference-time sparsity configurations. We train two Qwen2.5-7B models with different FFN sparsity levels (50% and 75%) and evaluate both across range of inference sparsities (30%, 50%, 75%, 90%). Figure 1b reports the average accuracy across the same six benchmarks reported in Table 3as function of FFN sparsity at inference time. Individual benchmark results are present in the Appendix Table 8. We observe an interesting behavior - models aligned with higher sparsity level yields more robust expert representations across inference sparsities. For example, the model trained at 75% sparsity consistently outperforms the 50% trained model across varying inference sparsities. This behavior can be explained by our reconstruction objective. When trained with fewer active experts, the model learns to encode information more efficiently within each expert, resulting in more compact and discriminative representations. Observation 3: Training at higher sparsity yields experts that generalize better across varying inference sparsities. 4.3.4. EFFECT OF INITIALIZATION ON TRAINING DYNAMICS We investigate how different expert construction strategies affect fine-tuning dynamics by comparing DOT-MoE, CMoE, and LLaMA-MoE-v2 on LLaMA-3 8B. All methods are trained on the same data split at 25% FFN sparsity. Figure 2 shows training loss, WikiText perplexity, and zero-shot HellaSwag normalized accuracy as function of training tokens. DOT-MoE exhibits clear advantage at initialization, starting with substantially lower training loss compared to CMoE and LLaMA-MoE-v2. While all methods reduce training loss over time, CMoE and LLaMA-MoE-v2 exhibit signs of overfitting: despite achieving lower training loss, they consistently show higher WikiText perplexity and lower HellaSwag accuracy compared to DOT-MoE. In contrast, DOT-MoE continues to improve on both validation perplexity and downstream task performance throughout training, demonstrating that its expert construction leads to more distinct and generalizable representations. These results show that output-aware expert construction provides not only better starting point but also more robust learning potential. The quality of the initial expert assignment directly impacts generalization, validating our approach of jointly optimizing neuron assignment and routing during the alignment phase. Observation 4: Output-aware initialization achieves superior training generalization whereas heuristic methods exhibit overfitting. 5. Future Work and Conclusion In this work, we introduced DOT-MoE, novel framework that formulates the conversion of dense FFNs into sparse MoEs as Differentiable Optimal Transport problem. By jointly learning the neuron-to-expert assignment and the routing policy via Straight-Through Estimators, we achieve superior trade-off between sparsity and performance compared to heuristic clustering or structured pruning methods. We further show that the same balanced-transport formulation generalizes to multi-head attention, yielding substantial gains when applied to attention-head assignment. Several 8 DOT-MoE: Differentiable Optimal Transport for MoEfication promising directions remain for future research. First, while we currently initialize the affinity matrix randomly, exploring data-driven initializations such as leveraging weight correlations or pre-computed activation statistics could accelerate Sinkhorn convergence and yield tighter clusters. Second, we plan to investigate the hard pruning of experts that exhibit consistently low utilization during training. Permanently removing these experts could reduce the models memory footprint beyond just inference FLOPs, bridging the gap between MoEfication and model compression.

sections.title: Impact Statement
sections.content: This paper presents work whose goal is to advance the field of Machine Learning by improving the inference efficiency of Large Language Models. By reducing the computational cost required for deployment, our method contributes to lowering the energy consumption and carbon footprint of foundation models. There are many potential societal consequences of our work, none of which we feel must be specifically highlighted here.

sections.title: Appendix
sections.content: A. The Assignment-Routing Gap The central claim of our method is that existing dense-toMoE approaches optimize inadequate proxies for the FFN output, whereas DOT-MoE is output-aware. We provide two complementary pieces of evidence: single-layer reconstruction analysis that isolates the assignment strategy under fixed evaluation protocol, and full-pipeline ablation that isolates it from training-recipe effects. A.1. Single-Layer Reconstruction We conduct controlled single-layer analysis that directly measures reconstruction fidelity under different expert assignment strategies. For each method, we compute the mean squared error (MSE) between the output of the original dense FFN and its sparse MoE approximation, isolating the effect of expert construction and routing. We analyze layer 31 of both LLaMA-2-7B and LLaMA-38B, partitioning the FFN intermediate dimension into experts of size = 128 and applying top-k = 10 routing. Expert assignments are constructed using calibration data from the WikiText training split, and reconstruction error is evaluated on the WikiText-2 test set. We compare four assignment strategies: LLaMA-MoE v1, LLaMA-MoE v2, CMoE, and our proposed DOT-MoE. All methods share identical expert granularity and routing configurations within each model. Table 4 shows consistent performance gap across both LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3. On LLaMA-2, LLaMA-MoE v1 incurs over 35 higher reconstruction error than DOT-MoE, LLaMA-MoE v2 nearly 9, and CMoE more than 2. The same pattern holds for LLaMA-3, where random and proxybased assignments yield substantially higher error, while DOT-MoE consistently achieves the lowest MSE. These results indicate that clustering neurons based on input-side statistics or intermediate activations is insufficient, and that preserving the FFN output requires explicitly modeling each neurons interaction with the down-projection and residual stream. Table 4. Single-layer reconstruction loss (MSE) on layer 31 for LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3. All models use = 128 neurons per expert and top-k = 10 routing. Calibration is performed on WikiText training data, and evaluation is conducted on WikiText-2. Lower is better. Method LLaMA-2 LLaMA-3 Error Rel. Error Rel. Output-Aware Assignment Ablation To further isolate the contribution of the OT-based assignment from training-recipe effects, we run CMoE and DOTMoE under an identical fine-tuning pipeline on Qwen2.5-7B at the same expert granularity (E=8, top-k=2), with the same data and training steps. Table 5 reports cosine similarity and MSE of hidden representations before and after the LM head against the dense teacher. Table 5. Numerical Stability We implement several techniques to ensure numerical stability during training: B.2.1. LOG-DOMAIN IMPLEMENTATION. For numerical stability, we implement Sinkhorn iterations in log-space (Algorithm 1). This avoids underflow when τ is small and enables stable computation even with thousands of neurons and experts. ASSIGNMENT LOGITS PRECISION. The assignment logits are maintained in FP32 regardless of model dtype to ensure stable Sinkhorn iterations. Router weights Wr use the models native dtype. B.2.3. TEMPERATURE ANNEALING. We linearly anneal the Sinkhorn temperature from τstart = 1.0 to τend = 0.1 during the warmup phase. Higher temperatures early in training allow exploration of the assignment space; lower temperatures sharpen assignments as training progresses. Validation always uses τend. C. Figure 3 shows the resulting visualization for layer 9, where each color represents different expert. The visualization reveals clear clustering structure, indicating that experts learn to specialize in processing distinct types of inputs. Activations from the same expert tend to cluster together in the embedding space, forming well-separated regions. The clear separation between expert clusters suggests that our method successfully partitions the representation space, with minimal redundancy across experts. E. Sparse MoE Computation During the alignment phase, we simulate sparse MoE computation without materializing separate expert weights. Given the neuron assignment and routing mask R, we compute the sparse MoE output by masking the intermediate activations: ˆY = (cid:0)H (RM)(cid:1)Wdown (16) where Rndffn is the intermediate activation from Eq. 1 and denotes element-wise multiplication. The matrix product RM {0, 1}ndffn composes two levels of selection: {0, 1}nE: which experts are active for each token {0, 1}Edffn : which neurons belong to each expert RM {0, 1}ndffn : which neurons are active for each token Since each token activates experts and each expert contains neurons, only out of dffn neurons contribute to each tokens output. This masking-based formulation enables efficient training on the original dense weights while simulating sparse computation. After alignment training converges, we extract the final binary assignment and use it to partition the dense FFN weights into separate expert modules. For each expert e, we slice the corresponding rows from Wgate, Wup, and columns from Wdown according to the neurons in cluster Ce = {i : Mi,e = 1}. The resulting model is standard MoE architecture compatible with existing sparse inference frameworks. F. Expert Specialization and Utilization Expert Specialization. To understand the learned behavior of our converted MoE model, we visualize expert output activations using t-SNE (Maaten & Hinton, 2008). Extension to Attention Layers DOT-MoE extends to multi-head attention by treating heads as the units to be grouped into experts, mirroring the FFN setting where neurons are grouped. Given Nh query heads of dimension dh, we form Eattn = Nh/sh experts of sh heads and activate kattn per token via separate router . learnable affinity matrix Aattn RNhEattn with Wattn marginals (1Nh , sh1Eattn) is optimized via the same logdomain Sinkhorn iterations and discretized through the same STE as Eq. 11. During training, all heads are computed with frozen dense weights; sparsity is realized by masking the concatenated head outputs before the output projection WO, yielding an expression identical in form to the FFN reconstruction (Eq. 16). For Grouped Query Attention (Ainslie et al., 2023), assignment operates at query-head granularity and all KV heads are computed unconditionally; sparsity therefore lives in the and projections, which dominate attention parameters. To validate the formulation, we evaluate attention-only DOT-MoE on Qwen2.5-7B at 50% attention sparsity (Nh=28, Eattn=14, sh=2, kattn=7) against random headassignment baseline with trained router under the same training recipe. Table 10 shows that OT-based assignment outperforms the baseline by +17.9 points on average (64.1 vs. 46.2). Two points are worth noting. First, the search space is far smaller than for the FFN (28 heads vs. 18,944 neurons), so attention assignment converges faster and with less gradient variance; the attention router itself matches the FFN router architecturally and adds negligible compute. Second, because attention parameters are roughly one third of FFN parameters, attention-only MoEfication yields modest overall compression; combining it with FFN MoEfication (joint MLP+Attention) is direct extension. H. Training Overhead We profile DOT-MoEs alignment phase on 8H100 to quantify the cost of Sinkhorn iterations and straight-through estimation relative to standard dense forward/backward pass. Sinkhorn iterations account for only 2% of the total forward-and-backward time. All DOT-MoE-specific operations combined add 15% overhead per training step over standard dense training step; most of this overhead comes from hard-assignment matrix construction rather than from Sinkhorn itself. We currently run the greedy rounding on CPU and incur CPU-to-GPU transfer overhead; dedicated GPU kernel (e.g., using parallel bucketing or priorityqueue primitive) would remove most of this cost. Crucially, this overhead is incurred only during the alignment phase: once alignment converges, the extracted MoE model is standard fused-expert architecture with no Sinkhorn or STE at inference time. I. Additional Dense-to-MoE Baselines I.1. Comparison with LTE LTE (Zheng et al., 2024) controls sparsity through scalar η and activates variable number of experts per token via sigmoid thresholding. To enable fair comparison, we evaluate DOT-MoE at matching FFN sparsity on LLaMA-27B with E=86 experts  (Table 9)  . At lower FFN sparsity (25% vs. 29%), DOT-MoE still outperforms LTE by +6.0 points on average. Beyond accuracy, LTEs sigmoid routing activates variable number of experts per token, leading to unpredictable per-token compute; DOT-MoE uses softmax top-k routing, which keeps compute per token constant and is compatible with standard fused-MoE serving kernels. Controlled Same-Granularity Comparison natural concern in dense-to-MoE comparisons is that different expert granularities can confound the contribution of the assignment strategy. To address this, we run DOTMoE at CMoEs own granularity (E=8, top-k=2) with 1.2B fine-tuning tokens across three model families. Table 12 reports the controlled comparison.
